text.title: Wireframing Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules every wireframing application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Wireframes MUST show structure, hierarchy, navigation, function, and interaction patterns. They MUST EXCLUDE colour (except greyscale), typography styles, real images, branding, and final copy.
rule.rationale: Visual styling commitments before structural validation conflate two distinct design phases and create premature attachment.

rule.statement: Every screen wireframe MUST cover ALL states: default, empty, loading, error, success, and any permission-gated view. Missing any state is a violation.
rule.rationale: Empty + error + permission states are always missed in design but always hit by users on first use.

rule.statement: Key screens MUST be wireframed in ≥3 layout variants before converging on one direction. Single-option reviews produce acceptance-without-exploration.
rule.rationale: Single-option reviews produce either rubber-stamp acceptance or rejection-without-alternatives.

rule.statement: Every wireframe MUST include mobile breakpoint notes, even for desktop-first products.
rule.rationale: Mobile is the dominant context; assuming desktop produces brittle responsive behaviour.

rule.statement: Every wireframe MUST include an annotation table covering per-element behaviour, conditional states, and technical requirements.
rule.rationale: Layouts without annotations defer behaviour decisions to implementation by accident.

rule.statement: Every wireframe document MUST include an "Open Questions" section surfacing design assumptions needing PM/owner sign-off before engineering begins.
rule.rationale: Open-question lists are the most valuable part of a wireframe doc for early-stage design; agents skip them unless prompted.

rule.statement: Annotation tables MUST be generated from the actual feature spec / acceptance criteria. Inventing plausible-sounding behaviour without spec context is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Without spec context, agents fabricate behaviours that look correct but mislead engineering.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
